page: Alibaba Cloud ecs.m1.xlarge is a general-purpose virtual machine instance belonging to the ecs.m1 server family. It is built on the x86_64 CPU architecture and runs on the KVM hypervisor. The instance features 8 dedicated physical cores with 1.0 thread per core, providing a total of 8 vCPUs. This dedicated CPU allocation ensures that compute resources are not shared with other tenants, offering consistent processing capabilities.

The instance is equipped with 32.0 GB of system memory, yielding a memory-to-core ratio of 4.0 GB per core. This balanced 1:4 resource ratio aligns with standard general-purpose configurations. The ecs.m1.xlarge does not include any bundled local storage or integrated GPUs, meaning storage requirements must be met via network-attached block storage options. There are also no complimentary public IPv4 addresses included with this instance type.

Given its balanced compute-to-memory ratio and dedicated CPU allocation, the ecs.m1.xlarge is suitable for general-purpose workloads. These include web serving, application servers, mid-range databases, and development or testing environments that require stable CPU performance without the need for local NVMe storage or hardware acceleration.
description: Alibaba Cloud ecs.m1.xlarge is a general-purpose virtual machine instance from the ecs.m1 family, built on the x86_64 architecture and managed via the KVM hypervisor. It features 8 dedicated physical cores with 1.0 thread per core, delivering 8 vCPUs of dedicated compute capacity. The instance is configured with 32.0 GB of RAM, establishing a balanced 1:4 vCPU-to-memory ratio of 4.0 GB per core. It does not include local storage or integrated GPUs, requiring network-attached storage for data persistence. With its dedicated CPU allocation and balanced resource profile, the ecs.m1.xlarge is designed for workloads such as web serving, application hosting, and moderate database operations. This instance type offers a stable hardware profile for environments requiring predictable CPU performance without the overhead of shared-core resource contention.
